result:
The View from the Edge of the Bed  
A 2500-word drift through the night, the self, the city, and the small, bright technologies that keep us awake

I.

It is 2:07 a.m. according to the cable box, which means it is 2:04 according to the stove, which means nothing at all according to the body that refuses to decide whether it is still yesterday or already tomorrow. I am sitting on the extreme periphery of a mattress that has outlived three romantic relationships, two presidential terms, and one cat. The sheets are bunched like failed origami; the blanket has surrendered to gravity and pools on the floor like a guilty conscience. I have been awake long enough to hear the fridge in the kitchen finish its entire cycle—compressor sighing, tray clinking, the small seismic shudder when the ice releases. The apartment is a modest metropolis of noises, each with a personality, each convinced I am listening only to it.

II.

When you are the only one awake in a building, you inherit the silence like a family estate. You stroll through rooms you did not build, trace banisters you did not sand, and feel the phantom weight of every previous tenant who ever stood barefoot on the same floorboards wondering why love had left or whether the rash on their arm was cancer or merely stress. Houses remember in the language of settling beams and expanding pipes; they keep our worst midnights inside their drywall like diaries hidden under loose floorboards. If a structure stands long enough, it becomes a palimpsest of panic attacks, orgasms, chili pots left overnight on the burner. I once found, behind the panel of a bathroom wall I opened to chase a leak, a child’s crayon inscription: “Kevin is king 1978.” I have no idea who Kevin was, but for a decade I brushed my teeth while his purple crown hovered inches from my shoulder, a ghost monarch presiding over my fluoride.

III.

Insomnia is the mind’s way of refusing to vacate the premises after the lease has expired. You keep turning the lights back on, clattering dishes, playing the Smiths at low volume because you do not want the neighbors to know you are lonely, but also you sort of do. The brain becomes a nightclub whose bouncer has quit; any thought can stroll in wearing whatever disreputable outfit it wants. Suddenly you are pondering the mortality of asparagus—how it wilts so quickly, how the tips liquefy into black slime if you do not use them in time—until the asparagus becomes a stand-in for every abandoned project, every friend you forgot to text back, every day you convinced yourself you would start fresh tomorrow. You recall that Joan Didion once wrote that she had “already lost the morning,” and you think: I have already lost the entire produce drawer.  

IV.

At 2:21 a.m. the phone on the nightstand lights up like a hand lifted in a classroom. It is not a call, just the reflex of a lithium-ion battery reorganizing its charge. Still, I pick it up the way an ex-smoker fingers an empty lighter. The lock screen is a photograph of my niece wearing a yellow slicker and holding a pinecone like a torch. She is four in the picture; she is eight now; she has probably forgotten the pinecone. Time is running a pyramid scheme and we are all at the bottom. I open the phone anyway, thumb through the apps the way a medieval monk might finger rosary beads—Twitter, Instagram, the weather in Kyoto, a game in which cartoon cats visit a virtual yard. The cats leave fake sardines as tips. The sardines buy prettier cushions. The cushions attract rarer cats. None of it matters, yet each ding of the notification bell releases a hit of dopamine so tiny it could be weighed only in micrograms, enough to keep a human creature tethered to a five-inch rectangle of glass that smells faintly of hand sanitizer and existential dread.

V.

Outside, the city is a woman who has removed her earrings but not her mascara. She has kicked off her heels and is standing on the fire escape smoking the last of the day’s cigarettes, watching squad cars scribble red loops across the lower sky. I know this because I go to the window. The sash sticks; I yank; it protests like a violin. Cold slips in, an impolite guest, and I welcome it because insomnia has made me a bad host to myself. Down on the avenue, a food-delivery cyclist pedals against the light, thermal bag on his back gleaming like a turtle shell. I imagine the warm dumplings inside, the little pleated moons steaming against the foil, the grateful recipient in plaid pajamas waiting for the buzzer. Once, when I could still sleep, I dreamed of dumplings. In the dream they floated like balloons and carried me over the East River. When I landed I was married to someone whose face I could not see; we grew tomatoes on a balcony and argued gently about whether basil preferred morning or afternoon sun. I awoke happy, then spent the day trying to recreate the recipe of the dream dumplings, failing, ordering them from a restaurant, falling asleep again before they arrived. The delivery person left them by the door; the rats got them; I found the plastic box gnawed into a shiv, little corrugations of teeth marks like graffiti that say: we were here, we were hungry, we took what you neglected.

VI.

There is a theory that every human has a certain number of heartbeats before the warranty expires, and that you can spend them fast—say, sprinting up stadium steps—or slow, say, meditating in a cave. Insomnia feels like a glitch in that contract: the ticker keeps drumming but the ledger fails to record any withdrawal, so you are left pacing the corridor of your own chest, convinced the total is being stolen from you in increments too small to measure. I once tried to test the theory with a fitness tracker, but the strap irritated my wrist and I kept waking up to hives shaped exactly like progress. The device would buzz softly whenever I hit ten thousand steps, even if those steps were the shuffle from bed to bathroom to kitchen to bed again, a pilgrim’s circuit of shame. One night it vibrated at 3:45 a.m. and informed me I had climbed the equivalent of twenty-two flights of stairs. I had not; I live on the fourth floor; the altimeter was confused by the way I kept lifting the phone above my face to doom-scroll. Still, the number glowed on the little OLED screen, triumphant. I felt absurdly proud, as if I had summited a metaphor without leaving the sheets.

VII.

The Japanese have a word, “tsundoku,” for the practice of buying books and letting them pile up unread. I have a private word—I call it “mukatsuku,” which I know is not a real Japanese word, it is closer to a slang for “irritating,” but I have hijacked it to mean the physical sensation of a book staring at you from the shelf with its unopened pages like eyes. At night the books become jury members. They murmur: you promised. You carried us home in crisp paper bags smelling of new ink, you laid us on the nightstand like newborn siblings, and still you scroll through remakes of sitcoms you hated the first time. The worst accuser is the translation of Proust I bought in 2011 after a breakup, convinced that seven volumes of madeleine and memory would cauterize the wound. The spine remains uncracked; the madeleines remain hypothetical; the wound learned to order take-out. Sometimes I take the book down, weigh it in my palms like a dumbbell, open it to the middle, read one sentence about asparagus—yes, asparagus again, Proust is haunted too—and close it, satisfied that literature has been served the way a child serves dinner to a doll: ceremoniously, and without nutrition.

VIII.

Memory itself is a kind of translation; each time you drag the file across the desktop of consciousness, something compresses, something else interpolates. The first time you remember your grandmother’s kitchen, you can still smell the soup. The hundredth time, you are remembering the remembering, a xerox of a xerox, edges blurring, soup turning into noodle, noodle into rice, rice into the white nothing between pixels. Scientists call this “reconsolidation,” which is a polite way of admitting that the past is a Google doc we keep editing in real time. My grandmother has been dead for nineteen years; her kitchen is now a nail salon; still, somewhere in the folds, I can summon the papery skin of her wrist when she ladled broth, the way she hummed a tune whose name neither of us knew. I do not know whether I preserve this moment or it preserves me. Perhaps we are two drunks propping each other up, memory and rememberer, staggering toward a future neither will survive.

IX.

At 3:03 a.m. the radiators begin their nightly opera: the aria of expanding iron, the hiss like a cymbal, the final thunk that sounds uncannily like someone sitting down on a bed that is not his own. I have lived with these sounds so long I incorporate them into dream fragments when sleep half arrives and then retreats like a tide ashamed of its own nakedness. Once, in the shallow end of a doze, I mistook the radiator’s clank for my father clearing his throat in the hallway, though he died before I ever rented this apartment. I called out “Dad?”—a word I rarely spoke aloud even when he was alive; our conversations happened in the auxiliary language of mowing lawns side by side, handing each other tools, the small mammalian silences of men who love each other but fear that saying so would break some ancient code. He did not answer, of course, but the radiator did, with a softer sigh, as if to say: I am only heat, I cannot parent you, please find a better placeholder for longing.

X.

Longing is a renewable resource; you can mine it all your life and never crest the ridge of want. Advertisers know this, which is why every subway poster addresses not your life but your life-adjacent self: the you who has whiter teeth, the you who booked the Maldives, the you who stands on escalators but never blocks traffic. The city runs on the gasoline of deferred satisfaction. I sometimes think the entire economy is a sublimation of our fear of stillness; if we ever paused to inventory the contents of our own chests, GDP would flatline. At 3:17 a.m. I watch a video in which a man power-washes a driveway; the water stripes the concrete from gray to darker gray; moss flees like embarrassed tourists; the comments are ecstatic: SO SATISFYING. I watch six more clips—grout cleaning, ear wax removal, pimple extraction—each a miniature redemption. We crave completion, a narrative arc even if it is only mildew surrendering to pressure. My own mildew is metaphysical; no nozzle exists that can blast it off.

XI.

I used to believe that adulthood would be a destination like terminus on a metro map: once arrived, the doors would ding open and I would step onto the platform carrying an umbrella of competence, a suitcase of purpose. Instead, adulthood keeps proving itself a movable feast, or perhaps an all-you-can-eat buffet that ran out of shrimp hours ago and now offers only cocktail sauce and resignation. I pay bills, I schedule physicals, I file taxes, yet inside I remain the kid who lost his kite in a tree and keeps returning to the same park expecting the kite to have crawled down and apologized. What no one tells you is that the kite was never the point; the point was the panic of looking up and seeing nothing but wind. The panic is portable; it fits in a wallet next to expired gym memberships; it flutters out at 3:22 a.m. and wraps itself around your throat like a fashion accessory from a dystopian runway.

XII.

To combat the flutter, I try lists. I list groceries: eggs, oat milk, something green I will forget to eat. I list errands: dry cleaner, pharmacy, the thank-you note I have owed my aunt since the Reagan administration. I list countries I will never visit: Moldova, Tonga, the micro-nation of San Marino which I picture as a single quaint piazza floating in space like an island in a Magritte painting. Lists give the illusion of cartography: here be dragons, here be fixed. The truth is the dragons migrated to a different hemisphere while you were alphabetizing spices. Still, I write them in the Notes app, each line a tiny paper crane I fold and release into the cloud, that vast attic where we store the furniture of our minds and hope it does not rot.

XIII.

Rot, though, is just another word for transformation with bad PR. The pear liquifying in my fruit bowl is becoming a microbrewery for fruit flies; they hover in their abbreviated lives, entire civilizations rising and falling between lunch and dinner. I envy them their brevity. Human years stretch like taffy; you stretch and stretch until you look down and notice holes poked through by dental bills, layoffs, the occasional planetary pandemic. Meanwhile the fruit flies have bankrupted themselves into compost and the universe has already cashed the check. I once read about a philosophical thought experiment called the “ticking time-slip,” which posits that if you could live forever, you would eventually run out of distinguishable experiences and enter a loop of perfect repetition, doomed to reenact every mistake an infinite number of times. Immortality, then, is simply insomnia with better marketing.

XIV.

At 3:29 the first bird begins. Not the confident pre-dawn chorus, just a single throat clearing in the dark—an avian insomniac, perhaps, or a sentry whose shift started early. I open the window wider; the cold flings itself against my chest like a child demanding to be carried. The bird is a robin, I think, though I can never keep them straight: sparrows, starlings, the occasional renegade cardinal. My ignorance of birds is a small shame I carry like a pebble in a boot; it nags, but not enough to impede motion. I once downloaded an app that promised to identify birdsong, but it mislabeled the robin as a nightjar and I lost faith in algorithms. Now I simply listen. The bird sings three notes, pauses, then three again. It could be Morse code for “help,” or “hello,” or “I am also alone.” I whistle back a poor imitation; it quiets, perhaps out of pity. A hush expands between us, me in my boxer shorts, the robin on a branch invisible to streetlights. For a moment the city is a photograph developing in reverse, fading into potential.

XV.

Potential is what you stockpile when the store has run out of present. I have banked so much potential I should qualify for interest. Novels unwritten, muscles un-ripped, soufflés un-collapsed. The trouble is that potential is a currency that devalues the longer you hoard it; spend it too early and you peak at twenty-five, spend it too late and you become the punchline of your own obituary: “died suddenly, had plans.” The bird sings again; I imagine it mocking my paralysis. It has already found a mate, built a nest, laid eggs the color of a sky we will never see again. By noon it will have flown more miles than I have walked this year. Yet even it will be eaten someday by a larger bird or a neighborhood cat or simply expire mid-flight, plummeting into traffic while commuters update podcasts. The difference is that the robin never mistook potential for identity; it simply sang until it couldn’t.

XVI.

I used to write poems at this hour, back when I still believed poems were a ticket out of the self rather than a tunnel deeper in. The paper would receive my dread like gauze; ink darkened, margin closed over the wound. I never showed those poems to anyone, partly out of modesty, mostly out of embarrassment. They were hysterical, self-pitying, the poetic equivalent of leaving a voicemail at 4:00 a.m. saying only “I just wanted to hear your voice.” One winter I burned them in the sink, page by page, because I read that Tibetan monks made sand mandalas and then swept them away, and I liked the symmetry of art disappearing into smoke. What I did not realize is that the monks work in community, their destruction a shared ritual, whereas I was alone with my laptop glowing like a deputy watching me commit arson on myself. The flames turned the words into tiny black butterflies that stained the porcelain; I scrubbed for days but the outline remained, a silhouette of every sentiment I could not bear to share.

XVII.

Sharing, in theory, is the antidote to shame, yet sharing is also a marketplace where shame is sometimes repackaged as content. I think of the influencers who livestream their panic attacks for empathy and ad revenue, who cry mascara into ring lights and monetize the drip. I judge them, then remember I have done lesser versions: the cryptic tweets, the sad selfie captioned with a joke, the fishing expedition for strangers to confirm I exist. We are all hawking souvenirs from the gift shop of our wounds. The difference is how transparent we are about the markup. At 3:36 a.m. I type “can’t sleep” into the search bar and watch the autofill suggestions: “can’t sleep after breakup,” “can’t sleep anxiety,” “can’t sleep spiritual warfare.” I add “can’t sleep bird singing,” half hoping someone has written a think-piece. The algorithm offers a Reddit thread from six years ago, a single reply: “robins start early when horny, good for them, go to bed.” I laugh out loud; the sound is unfamiliar, like finding an extra bulb in the junk drawer when you’ve already bought a new lamp.

XVIII.

Laughter, even solitary, is a solvent; it dissolves the shell of the self just enough to let oxygen in. I stand at the window and breathe the cold, feel it reach the corridors of the lungs where the day’s stale gossip still echoes. The sky has begun to lighten at the eastern edge, not yet color, just a slackening of dark, as if someone loosened the screw on a stage light. The bird has grown bolder; now it trills a longer phrase, then listens. Somewhere a second bird answers, farther away, like a friend acknowledging a text but refusing to leave the party early. I imagine them negotiating territory, boundaries, the small civilities that keep wars from starting. If only humans came with songs concise as passwords, calibrated to carry only across necessary distances. Instead we invent languages that metastasize into jargon, into terms and conditions, into declarations of independence that require translation into seventeen other tongues, each with its own slang for “I hurt.”

XIX.

Hurting, though, is just another word for living with awareness. Stones do not hurt; they do not possess nerves to register loss. The price of being hinged is that you open and close, you chafe, you rust. I return to bed, though I know sleep has already boarded a later train. The sheet is cold where I left it; I pull the blanket up to my ears like a child trying to muffle thunder. From here the ceiling looks like a sky we never evolved to see, stippled with plaster constellations. I trace shapes the way cavemen traced bulls: there the hunter, there the saucepan, there the ex-girlfriend who said I loved ideas more than people. She was not wrong; I hoard ideas the way dragons hoard gold, stacking them into glittering walls that keep the world out and the fire in. Yet ideas cool; they become the iron of radiators, clanking in the night, demanding maintenance.

XX.

Maintenance is the verb we conjugate in the imperative at 3:42 a.m.: fix the diet, fix the posture, fix the pension, fix whatever broke while you were busy being busy. I compile a mental syllabus: yoga on YouTube, Roth IRA, more water, less rage. The list calms me; it suggests a future in which I emerge lean and solvent, capable of small talk without rehearsing it first. The fantasy lasts until I remember that I have compiled the same syllabus every year since the iPhone was invented, and still I am here, hostage to a bird and a radiator. Perhaps the truest syllabus is shorter: breathe, exhale, repeat until the repetition becomes a song, until the song becomes a lullaby even a robin would recognize. The first sliver of actual color appears in the sky now, a bruise of rose beneath the charcoal. It is not beauty so much as permission: go on, palely, gently, into the version of yourself that wakes when you do.

XXI.

I decide to let the day begin without me. I will not contest the light; I will not barter for another hour. Instead I watch it seep across the room like a tide, touching the stack of laundry, the glass of water perspiring on the coaster, the mirror that shows a face I keep forgetting is mine because it changes when depleted. The eyes are red, the stubble older, the expression one of someone who has spent the night in customs with no stamp in his passport. Still, I offer the expression a truce: you kept vigil, you did not descend into the basement of despair, you listened to a bird and did not envy its wings enough to grow them wrong. That is something. Not redemption, perhaps, but the down payment.

XXII.

At 4:00 a.m. the building’s garbage truck arrives with the soundtrack of dinosaur bones. Metal jaws chew through plastic bags, compressing takeout containers into confessions. I hear glass break, a bottle that once held wine or kombucha or maybe both. The sound is bright, felonious. I imagine every discarded object telling its abbreviated memoir: I held merlot on the anniversary; I cradled soy sauce on the night he didn’t text back; I stored antibiotics that turned the tongue black. The truck drives away, burden heavier, cab lights flicking off like eyes closing after a long shift. I think of the landfill waiting to receive the cargo, that underground buffet where seagulls and rats hold conferences on what we choose to leave behind. We are all landlords of garbage; we merely prefer not to collect the rent.

XXIII.

Soon the upstairs neighbor’s alarm will go off, an old-fashioned bell that clangs like a fire engine. He is a baker; he told me once that he has kneaded every thought he ever had into dough, and by the time the loaves rise he no longer remembers what troubled him. I picture him now, padding across his floor in flour-dusted socks, switching on the shower, the water drumming the pipes like distant artillery. I picture the loaves in their pans, bellies swelling under cloth, the way time swells inside us until it must be punched down, allowed to rise again. I cannot bake; I have tried; the yeast senses my desperation and goes on strike. Still, I appreciate the metaphor: that we are all unfinished bread, perpetually in proof, never quite ready for the eternal oven.

XXIV.

By 4:15 a.m. the sky has upgraded from bruise to watercolor. A plane crosses the distance, red eye winking. I imagine the passengers inside, heads tilted against oval windows, mouths open like fish. Some are fleeing, some returning, some simply suspended between origin stories. I once took a red-eye and sat beside a woman who cried silently into a scarf the entire flight. I pretended not to notice, offering only the plastic cup of orange juice the attendant handed me. When we landed she turned and said, “Thank you for not asking,” then vanished into the jet bridge. I still think of her, the way her grief pressurized the cabin, the way we shared an armrest like a life raft. Perhaps she thinks of me too, the boy with orange juice who kept his questions caged. Perhaps we all carry small memories of strangers the way books carry pressed flowers—moments flattened to keep them from wilting, forgotten until the page is turned years later.

XXV.

I turn my own page now, not in sleep but in surrender. I will get up, shower, boil water for tea that I will forget to drink. I will answer emails with the phrase “circling back” as though any of us are horses in a paddock. I will do all of this on two hours of dreams snatched like purses. But for the moment, I remain horizontal, witness to the final phases of night’s disassembly. The robin has grown hoarse or perhaps has simply flown to breakfast. The radiator ticks its final tick. Somewhere a car alarm tests a four-note melody and quits, embarrassed. Light comes in full enough to etch the edges of the blinds, to make dust motes visible, galaxies in miniature. I study them the way I once studied planets in a textbook, convinced that if I learned their names they would grant me safe passage. Now I know better: the names are arbitrary, the passage is never safe, the dust returns to dust, but for now it dances, suspended in a shaft of sun that has traveled eight minutes to announce morning, that has, in some small measure, arrived on time even if I have not.

Once, in the shallow end of a doze, I mistook the radiator’s clank for my father clearing his throat in the hallway, though he died before I ever rented this apartment. I called out “Dad?”—a word I rarely spoke aloud even when he was alive; our conversations happened in the auxiliary language of mowing lawns side by side, handing each other tools, the small mammalian silences of men who love each other but fear that saying so would break some ancient code. He did not answer, of course, but the radiator did, with a softer sigh, as if to say: I am only heat, I cannot parent you, please find a better placeholder for longing.

X.

Longing is a renewable resource; you can mine it all your life and never crest the ridge of want. Advertisers know this, which is why every subway poster addresses not your life but your life-adjacent self: the you who has whiter teeth, the you who booked the Maldives, the you who stands on escalators but never blocks traffic. The city runs on the gasoline of deferred satisfaction. I sometimes think the entire economy is a sublimation of our fear of stillness; if we ever paused to inventory the contents of our own chests, GDP would flatline. At 3:17 a.m. I watch a video in which a man power-washes a driveway; the water stripes the concrete from gray to darker gray; moss flees like embarrassed tourists; the comments are ecstatic: SO SATISFYING. I watch six more clips—grout cleaning, ear wax removal, pimple extraction—each a miniature redemption. We crave completion, a narrative arc even if it is only mildew surrendering to pressure. My own mildew is metaphysical; no nozzle exists that can blast it off.

XI.

I used to believe that adulthood would be a destination like terminus on a metro map: once arrived, the doors would ding open and I would step onto the platform carrying an umbrella of competence, a suitcase of purpose. Instead, adulthood keeps proving itself a movable feast, or perhaps an all-you-can-eat buffet that ran out of shrimp hours ago and now offers only cocktail sauce and resignation. I pay bills, I schedule physicals, I file taxes, yet inside I remain the kid who lost his kite in a tree and keeps returning to the same park expecting the kite to have crawled down and apologized. What no one tells you is that the kite was never the point; the point was the panic of looking up and seeing nothing but wind. The panic is portable; it fits in a wallet next to expired gym memberships; it flutters out at 3:22 a.m. and wraps itself around your throat like a fashion accessory from a dystopian runway.

XII.

To combat the flutter, I try lists. I list groceries: eggs, oat milk, something green I will forget to eat. I list errands: dry cleaner, pharmacy, the thank-you note I have owed my aunt since the Reagan administration. I list countries I will never visit: Moldova, Tonga, the micro-nation of San Marino which I picture as a single quaint piazza floating in space like an island in a Magritte painting. Lists give the illusion of cartography: here be dragons, here be fixed. The truth is the dragons migrated to a different hemisphere while you were alphabetizing spices. Still, I write them in the Notes app, each line a tiny paper crane I fold and release into the cloud, that vast attic where we store the furniture of our minds and hope it does not rot.

XIII.

Rot, though, is just another word for transformation with bad PR. The pear liquifying in my fruit bowl is becoming a microbrewery for fruit flies; they hover in their abbreviated lives, entire civilizations rising and falling between lunch and dinner. I envy them their brevity. Human years stretch like taffy; you stretch and stretch until you look down and notice holes poked through by dental bills, layoffs, the occasional planetary pandemic. Meanwhile the fruit flies have bankrupted themselves into compost and the universe has already cashed the check. I once read about a philosophical thought experiment called the “ticking time-slip,” which posits that if you could live forever, you would eventually run out of distinguishable experiences and enter a loop of perfect repetition, doomed to reenact every mistake an infinite number of times. Immortality, then, is simply insomnia with better marketing.

XIV.

At 3:29 the first bird begins. Not the confident pre-dawn chorus, just a single throat clearing in the dark—an avian insomniac, perhaps, or a sentry whose shift started early. I open the window wider; the cold flings itself against my chest like a child demanding to be carried. The bird is a robin, I think, though I can never keep them straight: sparrows, starlings, the occasional renegade cardinal. My ignorance of birds is a small shame I carry like a pebble in a boot; it nags, but not enough to impede motion. I once downloaded an app that promised to identify birdsong, but it mislabeled the robin as a nightjar and I lost faith in algorithms. Now I simply listen. The bird sings three notes, pauses, then three again. It could be Morse code for “help,” or “hello,” or “I am also alone.” I whistle back a poor imitation; it quiets, perhaps out of pity. A hush expands between us, me in my boxer shorts, the robin on a branch invisible to streetlights. For a moment the city is a photograph developing in reverse, fading into potential.

XV.

Potential is what you stockpile when the store has run out of present. I have banked so much potential I should qualify for interest. Novels unwritten, muscles un-ripped, soufflés un-collapsed. The trouble is that potential is a currency that devalues the longer you hoard it; spend it too early and you peak at twenty-five, spend it too late and you become the punchline of your own obituary: “died suddenly, had plans.” The bird sings again; I imagine it mocking my paralysis. It has already found a mate, built a nest, laid eggs the color of a sky we will never see again. By noon it will have flown more miles than I have walked this year. Yet even it will be eaten someday by a larger bird or a neighborhood cat or simply expire mid-flight, plummeting into traffic while commuters update podcasts. The difference is that the robin never mistook potential for identity; it simply sang until it couldn’t.

XVI.

I used to write poems at this hour, back when I still believed poems were a ticket out of the self rather than a tunnel deeper in. The paper would receive my dread like gauze; ink darkened, margin closed over the wound. I never showed those poems to anyone, partly out of modesty, mostly out of embarrassment. They were hysterical, self-pitying, the poetic equivalent of leaving a voicemail at 4:00 a.m. saying only “I just wanted to hear your voice.” One winter I burned them in the sink, page by page, because I read that Tibetan monks made sand mandalas and then swept them away, and I liked the symmetry of art disappearing into smoke. What I did not realize is that the monks work in community, their destruction a shared ritual, whereas I was alone with my laptop glowing like a deputy watching me commit arson on myself. The flames turned the words into tiny black butterflies that stained the porcelain; I scrubbed for days but the outline remained, a silhouette of every sentiment I could not bear to share.

XVII.

Sharing, in theory, is the antidote to shame, yet sharing is also a marketplace where shame is sometimes repackaged as content. I think of the influencers who livestream their panic attacks for empathy and ad revenue, who cry mascara into ring lights and monetize the drip. I judge them, then remember I have done lesser versions: the cryptic tweets, the sad selfie captioned with a joke, the fishing expedition for strangers to confirm I exist. We are all hawking souvenirs from the gift shop of our wounds. The difference is how transparent we are about the markup. At 3:36 a.m. I type “can’t sleep” into the search bar and watch the autofill suggestions: “can’t sleep after breakup,” “can’t sleep anxiety,” “can’t sleep spiritual warfare.” I add “can’t sleep bird singing,” half hoping someone has written a think-piece. The algorithm offers a Reddit thread from six years ago, a single reply: “robins start early when horny, good for them, go to bed.” I laugh out loud; the sound is unfamiliar, like finding an extra bulb in the junk drawer when you’ve already bought a new lamp.

XVIII.

Laughter, even solitary, is a solvent; it dissolves the shell of the self just enough to let oxygen in. I stand at the window and breathe the cold, feel it reach the corridors of the lungs where the day’s stale gossip still echoes. The sky has begun to lighten at the eastern edge, not yet color, just a slackening of dark, as if someone loosened the screw on a stage light. The bird has grown bolder; now it trills a longer phrase, then listens. Somewhere a second bird answers, farther away, like a friend acknowledging a text but refusing to leave the party early. I imagine them negotiating territory, boundaries, the small civilities that keep wars from starting. If only humans came with songs concise as passwords, calibrated to carry only across necessary distances. Instead we invent languages that metastasize into jargon, into terms and conditions, into declarations of independence that require translation into seventeen other tongues, each with its own slang for “I hurt.”

XIX.

Hurting, though, is just another word for living with awareness. Stones do not hurt; they do not possess nerves to register loss. The price of being hinged is that you open and close, you chafe, you rust. I return to bed, though I know sleep has already boarded a later train. The sheet is cold where I left it; I pull the blanket up to my ears like a child trying to muffle thunder. From here the ceiling looks like a sky we never evolved to see, stippled with plaster constellations. I trace shapes the way cavemen traced bulls: there the hunter, there the saucepan, there the ex-girlfriend who said I loved ideas more than people. She was not wrong; I hoard ideas the way dragons hoard gold, stacking them into glittering walls that keep the world out and the fire in. Yet ideas cool; they become the iron of radiators, clanking in the night, demanding maintenance.

XX.

Maintenance is the verb we conjugate in the imperative at 3:42 a.m.: fix the diet, fix the posture, fix the pension, fix whatever broke while you were busy being busy. I compile a mental syllabus: yoga on YouTube, Roth IRA, more water, less rage. The list calms me; it suggests a future in which I emerge lean and solvent, capable of small talk without rehearsing it first. The fantasy lasts until I remember that I have compiled the same syllabus every year since the iPhone was invented, and still I am here, hostage to a bird and a radiator. Perhaps the truest syllabus is shorter: breathe, exhale, repeat until the repetition becomes a song, until the song becomes a lullaby even a robin would recognize. The first sliver of actual color appears in the sky now, a bruise of rose beneath the charcoal. It is not beauty so much as permission: go on, palely, gently, into the version of yourself that wakes when you do.

XXI.

I decide to let the day begin without me. I will not contest the light; I will not barter for another hour. Instead I watch it seep across the room like a tide, touching the stack of laundry, the glass of water perspiring on the coaster, the mirror that shows a face I keep forgetting is mine because it changes when depleted. The eyes are red, the stubble older, the expression one of someone who has spent the night in customs with no stamp in his passport. Still, I offer the expression a truce: you kept vigil, you did not descend into the basement of despair, you listened to a bird and did not envy its wings enough to grow them wrong. That is something. Not redemption, perhaps, but the down payment.

XXII.

At 4:00 a.m. the building’s garbage truck arrives with the soundtrack of dinosaur bones. Metal jaws chew through plastic bags, compressing takeout containers into confessions. I hear glass break, a bottle that once held wine or kombucha or maybe both. The sound is bright, felonious. I imagine every discarded object telling its abbreviated memoir: I held merlot on the anniversary; I cradled soy sauce on the night he didn’t text back; I stored antibiotics that turned the tongue black. The truck drives away, burden heavier, cab lights flicking off like eyes closing after a long shift. I think of the landfill waiting to receive the cargo, that underground buffet where seagulls and rats hold conferences on what we choose to leave behind. We are all landlords of garbage; we merely prefer not to collect the rent.

XXIII.

Soon the upstairs neighbor’s alarm will go off, an old-fashioned bell that clangs like a fire engine. He is a baker; he told me once that he has kneaded every thought he ever had into dough, and by the time the loaves rise he no longer remembers what troubled him. I picture him now, padding across his floor in flour-dusted socks, switching on the shower, the water drumming the pipes like distant artillery. I picture the loaves in their pans, bellies swelling under cloth, the way time swells inside us until it must be punched down, allowed to rise again. I cannot bake; I have tried; the yeast senses my desperation and goes on strike. Still, I appreciate the metaphor: that we are all unfinished bread, perpetually in proof, never quite ready for the eternal oven.

XXIV.

By 4:15 a.m. the sky has upgraded from bruise to watercolor. A plane crosses the distance, red eye winking. I imagine the passengers inside, heads tilted against oval windows, mouths open like fish. Some are fleeing, some returning, some simply suspended between origin stories. I once took a red-eye and sat beside a woman who cried silently into a scarf the entire flight. I pretended not to notice, offering only the plastic cup of orange juice the attendant handed me. When we landed she turned and said, “Thank you for not asking,” then vanished into the jet bridge. I still think of her, the way her grief pressurized the cabin, the way we shared an armrest like a life raft. Perhaps she thinks of me too, the boy with orange juice who kept his questions caged. Perhaps we all carry small memories of strangers the way books carry pressed flowers—moments flattened to keep them from wilting, forgotten until the page is turned years later.

XXV.

I turn my own page now, not in sleep but in surrender. I will get up, shower, boil water for tea that I will forget to drink. I will answer emails with the phrase “circling back” as though any of us are horses in a paddock. I will do all of this on two hours of dreams snatched like purses. But for the moment, I remain horizontal, witness to the final phases of night’s disassembly. The robin has grown hoarse or perhaps has simply flown to breakfast. The radiator ticks its final tick. Somewhere a car alarm tests a four-note melody and quits, embarrassed. Light comes in full enough to etch the edges of the blinds, to make dust motes visible, galaxies in miniature. I study them the way I once studied planets in a textbook, convinced that if I learned their names they would grant me safe passage. Now I know better: the names are arbitrary, the passage is never safe, the dust returns to dust, but for now it dances, suspended in a shaft of sun that has traveled eight minutes to announce morning, that has, in some small measure, arrived on time even if I have not.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
